questions.text: A student writes the following line in Python: result = input("Enter a score: ") and then tries to calculate result + 10. The program crashes. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: input() returns a string, so adding an integer without converting first causes a TypeError.
Python cannot store numbers entered by the user.
The print() function is missing from the program.
The variable name result is a reserved keyword in Python.

questions.text: Python is described as an interpreted language, while C++ is typically compiled. Which statement best describes a key trade-off of Python's interpreted approach?

options.text: Interpreted languages are always less suited for AI work than compiled ones.
Python code cannot be shared across different operating systems.
Python is slower and also harder to read than compiled languages.
Python runs slower than compiled languages, but provides instant feedback that accelerates learning and development.

questions.text: You are building a program that stores a student's percentage grade, such as 87.5. Which data type should you use, and why?

options.text: bool, because the student either passes or fails.
float, because the value contains a decimal point and requires precision.
int, because grades are numbers and Python converts them automatically.
str, because grades are displayed as text on a report card.

questions.text: In Python, indentation is not just a style preference — it is part of the language's syntax. What happens if indentation is missing or inconsistent inside an if block?

options.text: Only the first missing indentation is flagged; the rest are ignored.
Python automatically adds the correct indentation and runs the program.
Python raises an IndentationError and stops execution.
The program runs but produces wrong output silently.

questions.text: A developer wants to share a Python notebook with a classmate who has no software installed on their computer. Which environment is the best choice, and what is its primary advantage?

options.text: Anaconda, because it installs all libraries at once without any browser.
Google Colab, because it runs in any browser, requires zero installation, and auto-saves to Google Drive.
Local Jupyter, because it works offline and gives full control over the file system.
VS Code, because it is the only IDE that supports Python notebooks.

questions.text: Examine this code: print("2026", "03", "30", sep="/"). What will be printed?
A programmer writes: total = 10 / 2. A colleague says the result is an integer 5. Is the colleague correct, and why?

options.text: No — the / operator always returns a float in Python, so the result is 5.0.
No — the result depends on which Python version is installed.
Yes — dividing two integers always returns an integer in Python.
Yes — Python removes the decimal automatically when the result is a whole number.

questions.text: Which of the following variable names violates Python's naming rules and would cause a SyntaxError?

A Python script includes several lines with # at the start, written in plain English explaining what each section does. A junior developer removes them, saying they slow the program down. Is this decision correct?

options.text: No — Python completely ignores comments at runtime, so they have zero effect on speed.
No — removing comments causes a SyntaxError if they contained important instructions.
Yes — comments are executed by Python, so removing them improves performance.
Yes — comments are only useful during development and should not be in final code.

questions.text: A student writes name = "Python" and then prints name[1]. They expect the output to be "P" because it is the first letter. What is actually printed, and why?

options.text: An error — square brackets cannot be used on string variables.
"P" — string indexing in Python starts at 1, so name[1] returns the first character.
"Py" — Python returns a two-character slice when a single index is used.
"y" — string indexing starts at 0, so name[1] refers to the second character.

data.name: Week 2 - Taking Control of Data & Logic

questions.text: What is the result of 10 / 2 in Python?
Which operator is used to find the remainder of a division?
What is the output of print("Hi" * 3)?
Which escape sequence is used to add a new line in a string?
How do you correctly use an f-string to print a variable named "price"?
What data type does the input() function always return?
What is the result of int(7.9) in Python?
Which operator is used to check if two values are equal?
In an "and" operation, when is the result True?

options.text: If at least one condition is True
If the first condition is True

questions.text: What is the standard number of spaces used for indentation in Python?
Which loop is best used when you know the exact number of items to iterate over?
What is the result of range(5)?
What keyword is used to exit a loop prematurely?
What happens if code under a 'for' loop is NOT indented?
Which keyword is used to define a custom reusable block of logic?
What is the difference between Parameters and Arguments?

options.text: Arguments are used in the 'def' line
Parameters are actual data; Arguments are placeholders
Parameters are placeholders; Arguments are actual data

questions.text: If a function uses 'print' instead of 'return', what value is sent back to the main program?
In a try/except block, where should you place the code that might crash?
What is 'Stack Unwinding'?

options.text: Python searching backward for a safety net (except block)

questions.text: What does the 'DRY' principle stand for?
Which bracket is used to define a List in Python?
What is the index of the first item in a Python List?
What is the result of [10, 20, 30, 40][1:3]?
Which data structure stores information in Key-Value pairs?
Why is the .get() method safer than using square brackets for Dictionaries?

options.text: It prevents a KeyError crash by returning a default value

questions.text: What is the main characteristic of a Set?

options.text: It keeps items in alphabetical order
It only stores unique items and automatically removes duplicates

questions.text: Which method is used to get both Keys and Values while looping through a Dictionary?
What happens if you modify a List inside a function?

options.text: Nothing, the original list stays the same
The original list is permanently changed because it's passed by reference

questions.text: Which collection would you choose to store a unique list of 'Banned User IDs' for fast lookup?
In System Architecture, what is 'Global State'?

options.text: Data that persists throughout the program's lifetime, usually defined outside functions

options.text: Data that persists throughout the program's lifetime, usually defined outside functions

questions.text: Which approach allows a computer to find its own rules from data?
Which step comes first in the ML lifecycle?
What does .argmax() return?

options.text: The index of the maximum value

questions.text: Normalization in AI usually involves scaling data to which range?
Why do we use cloud tools like Google Colab for AI?

options.text: To run heavy calculations on remote powerful servers
To save battery on our local computer

questions.text: What defines a 'Robust' model?

options.text: One that has a small file size
One that is written in many languages
One that only works for the developer
One that works consistently across different conditions and people

questions.text: A 'Deepfake' is an ethical concern primarily related to:
What is 'Feature Extraction'?

options.text: Buying new features for a model
Identifying unique mathematical markers in data

questions.text: AI Modalities like CV and NLP aim to simulate:
What is the main difference between Generative AI and Classification AI?

options.text: Generative AI creates new content, while Classification AI labels existing data
Generative AI is faster than Classification AI
Generative AI only works with images, while Classification AI works with text
Generative AI stores data, while Classification AI deletes data

data.name: Week 6 - Introduction to Data Science & Data Analysis

questions.text: Netflix invested $100 million in 'House of Cards' without a pilot episode. Why was this considered a data-driven decision rather than a guess?

options.text: The executives relied on the instinct of experienced producers who had a strong track record
They analysed viewing patterns, actor preferences, and director engagement of 30 million subscribers to identify a statistically reliable pattern before committing
They conducted a large audience survey before committing to production
They copied a successful model that had already worked on a competing platform

questions.text: A Data Scientist designs a recommendation model. An ML Engineer then takes over. What does the ML Engineer do that the Data Scientist does NOT?

options.text: The ML Engineer evaluates whether the model's accuracy is high enough for production
The ML Engineer selects which algorithm to use for the model
The ML Engineer takes the designed model and deploys it into a live system capable of serving millions of users every day
The ML Engineer trains the model on the raw dataset

questions.text: Twitter generates approximately 500 million tweets every day. Why does processing this data require a network of computers rather than a single powerful machine?

options.text: A network is needed only because tweets contain images and video, which are large files
A single machine could handle it, but using a network is cheaper
Legal regulations require data to be split across multiple servers in different countries
The volume, speed, and scale of the data exceeds what any single machine can store or process, so the workload must be distributed across many connected nodes

questions.text: What would most likely happen if a data scientist built a model (Step 5) immediately after collecting data (Step 2), skipping the Clean Data and Exploration steps?

options.text: The Deploy step would detect the problem and refuse to publish the model
The Evaluate step would automatically correct any errors introduced by skipping cleaning
The model would train faster and give the same results
The model would train on dirty, unexamined data — errors, duplicates, and missing values would distort the learned patterns, producing unreliable predictions

questions.text: How does Data Analysis relate to Data Science?

options.text: Data Analysis and Data Science are completely separate fields with no overlap
Data Analysis is a specialisation within the broader field of Data Science — it focuses on understanding past data, while Data Science also includes prediction, machine learning, and engineering
Data Science deals only with large datasets, while Data Analysis works with small spreadsheets
Data Science is a subset of Data Analysis; every Data Scientist is also a Data Analyst

questions.text: A shoe retailer wants to know which shoe size to stock the most. They have sales records for 1,000 customers. Which measure of centre is most directly useful here, and why?

options.text: Mean, because it averages all sizes sold and points to the middle of demand
Median, because half the customers wear a size below it and half above
Mode, because the most frequently purchased size tells the retailer exactly which size to order in the largest quantity
Variability, because the spread of sizes tells the retailer how many different sizes to stock

questions.text: An analyst needs to process 1.5 million rows of transaction data and run the same analysis automatically every morning. Which tool is most appropriate, and why?

options.text: Excel, because it is the most widely used tool in business and supports all standard formulas
Google Sheets, because it stores data in the cloud and can be shared with the whole team
Python, because it handles millions of rows efficiently, supports scheduled automation, and can run the entire pipeline without manual intervention
Tableau, because it creates the clearest visualisations from large datasets

questions.text: After applying =MAX and =MIN to the Science scores column, you get 98 and 22. What does this pair of values tell you that the average alone cannot?

options.text: It confirms that the average is accurate and no outliers exist
It identifies which specific student scored the highest and which scored the lowest
It reveals the full range of performance — the best and worst individual scores — indicating that the class has extremely uneven results that the average masks
It shows the total number of students who passed the Science exam

questions.text: After building the Bar Chart of subject averages, why is it faster to identify the strongest subject visually than by reading the three average numbers in cells?

options.text: Charts are faster only when the values are very close together, not when they differ significantly
The brain processes relative bar heights pre-attentively — the tallest bar is identified almost instantly without arithmetic, whereas comparing three numbers still requires mental calculation
The chart recalculates the averages more accurately than the AVERAGE formula
The chart sorts the subjects alphabetically, making comparison easier

questions.text: The DS Workflow's 'Monitor' step shows that a spam filter's accuracy has been steadily dropping over three months. What does this trigger, and why does it illustrate that the Workflow is a loop rather than a straight line?

options.text: Declining accuracy signals that the world has changed and the model no longer fits — the team loops back to 'Ask a Question', gathers fresh data, re-cleans, rebuilds, re-evaluates, and redeploys, showing the Workflow is a continuous improvement cycle
The Evaluate step is rerun on the existing model without collecting new data
The Monitor step automatically retrains the model without human involvement
The team should shut down the model and build a completely new product from scratch

questions.text: A student finds that her school's competition results were collected on paper, WhatsApp, and a Google Sheet — and nobody can read the combined data. What is the first step of the Data Wrangling pipeline she should start with?
Which of the following is NOT one of the three pillars of data quality?
A roster shows 'مهاجم', 'Forward', 'FWD', and 'forward' all meaning the same position. A player also appears twice with slightly different name spelling. Which two problem types are present?
An analyst receives a CSV file of player ages. One entry reads 'خمستاشر' (Arabic for fifteen) instead of 15. Which problem type is this, and which cleaning step fixes it?

options.text: Wrong Data Type — convert to integer

questions.text: A data analyst is deciding whether to drop or fill rows with missing phone numbers in a student dataset. She checks what the data will be used for before deciding. This reflects which core principle of data cleaning?

options.text: Always remove rows with any missing field
Missing values should always be replaced with zero
Only rows with all fields present are valid
The right fix depends on the intended use of the data

questions.text: Which data source type sends continuous real-time updates without the system needing to request them each time?
The Gather step of the Data Wrangling pipeline is complete. What is the correct next step?
Spotify enriches your listening history with genre tags and mood scores before its recommendation engine uses the data. Which pipeline step does this enrichment represent, and what comes after it?

A dataset passes the Accuracy and Completeness checks but fails Consistency — the same city appears as 'Alex', 'Alexandria', and 'الإسكندرية'. Which cleaning operation addresses the Consistency failure?
Which statement best describes why Data Wrangling matters in real-world systems like food delivery apps and sports associations?

options.text: It converts all data into CSV format
It ensures the data used for decisions is accurate, complete, and consistent
It replaces the need for Data Science

data.name: Week 8 – Introduction to the Web and HTML

questions.text: Which of the following correctly traces the step-by-step path of a web request?

options.text: Browser Request -> Internet Cables -> Server Processing -> HTTP Response Back to Browser.
HTML File Storage -> Client Browser -> Local Network Cable Routing.
HTTP Response -> Client Machine -> Internet Hardware Connection.
Server Database -> Browser Layout Rendering -> URL Generation.

questions.text: A developer wants to build a single text block containing a title that stands out, followed by a sentence. What is the standard structural layout?

options.text:
Embed a

element directly inside the opening bracket of an

tag.

Place an

to

heading tag first, followed by a separate

paragraph tag.
Use a <body> tag for the heading and a <head> tag for the sentence text.

Wrap everything inside a single

tag.

questions.text: If you notice that some text on your webpage is running into an incorrect style layout, which nesting pattern should you audit for errors?
What will happen if an HTML developer forgets to include a closing tag (e.g., leaving a <strong> tag open)?

options.text: The browser will automatically create a CSS file to fix the problem.
The text formatting or style layout will break and bleed down into the rest of the page elements.
The web browser will instantly crash and throw a fatal compilation error.
The website will fail to download from the web server entirely.

questions.text: Which components are absolutely necessary to build a valid, modern, and readable HTML document skeleton?

options.text: A long URL address, an interactive JavaScript function, and multiple image source files.
An HTTP Request block, a database server link, and five distinct heading elements.
<!DOCTYPE html> declaration, <html> root wrapper, <head> for metadata, and <body> for viewable content with clean indentation.
Only a <body> tag wrapped around CSS layout variable patterns.

questions.text: When you enter a website URL in the browser, what is the first step that usually happens

options.text: The browser converts HTML into CSS
The domain name is translated into an IP address using DNS
The website is stored in the browser cache

questions.text: Which HTML element is used to define a navigation section in a webpage
Why is semantic HTML important in web development

options.text: It improves accessibility and SEO understanding
It makes the page load faster automatically
It prevents the use of JavaScript
It replaces the need for CSS

questions.text: What is the main purpose of an HTTP request in web communication

options.text: To send and receive data between browser and server

questions.text: What does the browser build first after parsing HTML code

data.name: Week 9 – Building with HTML Tags and Elements

questions.text: A developer is building a recipe page and needs to present the cooking steps so that users follow them in sequence. Which HTML list element is the most semantically correct choice?

options.text: <dl> — definition lists are designed for step-based content
<li> — a single list item can hold all steps as nested content
<ol> — ordered lists communicate sequence and numbered steps
<ul> — it is the default list element for all content

questions.text: A project folder has: index.html at the root and about/team.html in a subfolder. From index.html, which href value correctly links to team.html using a relative path?
A developer adds to a page but omits the alt attribute. What accessibility problem does this create?

options.text: Screen readers cannot describe the image to visually impaired users — the alt attribute provides the text alternative
The browser requests the image twice because the alt attribute controls caching
The image will not render in any browser without an alt attribute
There is no problem — alt is optional and has no impact on accessibility

questions.text: A pricing comparison table has a cell that should span 2 columns. Which attribute and value achieves this?
A login form collects an email address. The developer uses <input type='text'> instead of <input type='email'>. What is the practical consequence?

options.text: The browser skips built-in email format validation — invalid addresses like 'hello' are accepted without warning
The form cannot be submitted when using type='text' for emails
The input will not render — type='email' is required for email fields
There is no difference — all input types store and send data identically

questions.text: A developer needs users to select their country from a list. Which combination of HTML elements is the correct approach?
A developer wants to add a visible title above a table that is semantically associated with it. Which HTML approach is correct?

options.text: <caption> placed as the first child inside <table> — it is the dedicated element for table titles
<h2> placed immediately before the <table> opening tag — headings associate with the next element automatically
<label for='table1'> placed outside the table with a matching id
<title> placed inside <table> — the title element works for both documents and tables

questions.text: An image tag reads: . The original photo is 400px wide. What visual problem occurs on screen?

options.text: The browser ignores the width attribute and renders the image at its natural size
The image is cropped to 800px and the rest is discarded
The image is hidden because the width exceeds the viewport
The image is stretched horizontally and appears distorted because the height is not adjusted proportionally

questions.text: A student wraps an tag inside an tag. What is the result in the browser?

options.text: The code is invalid — only text may appear between <a> tags
The image becomes a clickable link that navigates to the href destination
The image disappears — <a> suppresses non-text children
The image renders but the link only activates on the alt text area

questions.text: A student nests an <ol> inside a <li> of a <ul>. The inner <ol> lists three substeps. Which statement correctly describes this markup pattern?

options.text: It is invalid HTML — list elements cannot be nested inside each other
It is valid but only if the nested list has exactly the same number of items as the outer list
It is valid nested list markup — an <ol> or <ul> may appear as a direct child of <li>
It is valid only if the outer list is also an <ol>

data.name: Week 10 – CSS: Making Web Pages Look Great

questions.text: What is the main difference between HTML and CSS roles on a web page?

options.text: HTML controls data storage; CSS handles data transfer.
HTML defines content and structure; CSS handles presentation and styling.
HTML makes pages interactive; CSS updates databases.
They perform identical functions within a web page script.

questions.text: Reducing risk to the lowest reasonable level, rather than removing it completely, is the goal of:
